## [00:00 - 02:41]

When I'm giving presentations or talks, all of a sudden I get nervous and my voice starts to shake. I still know the content, but yet, oh, that just happens to me. So I'm assuming more practice, but also if you had other tips. Why do you think it's not shaking right now? Good point. Maybe I'm hearing from friends. If you feel more comfortable, it's low stakes. The pressure is on me and there's not as much pressure on you. Is that fair to say? Yes, I'm not the expert here. All right, I've got a couple of things I'd love to share with you, Lynette. The first thing I'll say, and I want you all to try this, hold your hand up like this. Okay, now I want you to tense it really hard. Tense it really hard. Tense it really hard. What happens when you tense it really hard? What happens to your hand? It shakes. When we're under pressure, what happens to our voice, facial expressions and our body, is we tense. And when we tense up with our body, all of a sudden it causes our body to vibrate. And when your body vibrates, it's because you're really tense. So first of all, you've got to understand the best way to relax your body is by breathing. I'll give you five tips. The first tip I'd give you Lynette is before you go out and you do this presentation in person or virtually, I would encourage you to do three cycles of Wim Hof breathing. It's called guided bubble breathing by Wim Hof. I promise it's not a cult. It's just a way to get lots of oxygen into your body. And by oxygenating your body, it starts to relax your mind and your body. So you're not so tense. And then if your body is not really tense, then you won't shake. If you can get your breathing control, that should cure that completely. If it doesn't, if your voice is still shaking, that means your vocal muscles are tense and you have to relax that. So the way to relax your vocal muscles, and I want you all to do this with me as well. I just want you to, I just want you to yawn. Just do a loud audible yawn. Just go, wonderful way to relax some of those vocal muscles there. Third thing you can do, this is something I learned in the world of theater. One of the biggest muscles in your mouth is your tongue. So you want to move your tongue around your mouth while keeping your mouth closed. So you do this. You do that 21 times to the right, 21 times to the left. And if you're doing this for the first time, it may get a little bit sore for you. But again, that just starts to relax your tongue a little. I suffered from this when I started being an Asian kid growing up in a small community to throwing me on stage, speaking from a thousands of people, definitely had the shakes. Next thing, this is for your articulators, your mouth. You want to make these sounds. You want to make, you want to say, ee, o, ee, o. Continue that for at least a minute and that relaxes your facial muscles. The final thing I'll say is add more volume. And as you add more volume, you relax your body, you relax your mind, you relax all of the muscles. It allows you to get rid of the majority of it.
